front: Concept: the irreducible properties that make a thing a particular individual rather than a member of a class, often translated as 'thisness'
Concept: C.S. Peirce used this Scholastic term to outline how we perceive events as separate and experience 'secondness'
Concept: the doctrine that properties like 'goodness' mean the same when applied to God as to creatures, differing only in degree rather than meaning
Philosopher: the Radical Orthodox School (Catherine Pickstock, John Milbank) blames this thinker for ushering in modernity through his key metaphysical concept
Work: compiled from Oxford lectures, this commentary on Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences introduces both haecceity and univocity of being
Philosopher: Gerard Manley Hopkins drew on this thinker's concept of haecceity to formulate his ideas of inscape and instress
Philosopher: a poem about this thinker's 'Oxford' describes the city as 'Towery' and 'cuckoo-echoing, bell-swarmed' and concludes 'he fired France for Mary without spot'
Philosopher: defended the Immaculate Conception six centuries before the Church made it doctrine; theorized Christ's body did not corrupt because every person of the Trinity is formally distinct
Philosopher: Martin Heidegger thought he was writing his Habilitation thesis on this thinker, but the text De modis significandi was actually by Thomas of Erfurt
Philosopher: argued that angels have individual, incomplete knowledge as a sign of their haecceity, contra Aquinas who claimed angels already know everything
